---
title: GitHub Flavored Markdown Spec
version: 0.29
date: '2019-04-06'
license: '[CC-BY-SA 4.0](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)'
...

# Introduction

## What is GitHub Flavored Markdown?

GitHub Flavored Markdown, often shortened as GFM, is the dialect of Markdown
that is currently supported for user content on GitHub.com and GitHub
Enterprise.

This formal specification, based on the CommonMark Spec, defines the syntax and
semantics of this dialect.

GFM is a strict superset of CommonMark. All the features which are supported in
GitHub user content and that are not specified on the original CommonMark Spec
are hence known as **extensions**, and highlighted as such.

While GFM supports a wide range of inputs, it's worth noting that GitHub.com
and GitHub Enterprise perform additional post-processing and sanitization after
GFM is converted to HTML to ensure security and consistency of the website.

## What is Markdown?

Markdown is a plain text format for writing structured documents,
based on conventions for indicating formatting in email
and usenet posts.  It was developed by John Gruber (with
help from Aaron Swartz) and released in 2004 in the form of a
[syntax description](http://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/syntax)
and a Perl script (`Markdown.pl`) for converting Markdown to
HTML.  In the next decade, dozens of implementations were
developed in many languages.  Some extended the original
Markdown syntax with conventions for footnotes, tables, and
other document elements.  Some allowed Markdown documents to be
rendered in formats other than HTML.  Websites like Reddit,
StackOverflow, and GitHub had millions of people using Markdown.
And Markdown started to be used beyond the web, to author books,
articles, slide shows, letters, and lecture notes.

What distinguishes Markdown from many other lightweight markup
syntaxes, which are often easier to write, is its readability.
As Gruber writes:

> The overriding design goal for Markdown's formatting syntax is
> to make it as readable as possible. The idea is that a
> Markdown-formatted document should be publishable as-is, as
> plain text, without looking like it's been marked up with tags
> or formatting instructions.
> (<http://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/>)

The point can be illustrated by comparing a sample of
[AsciiDoc](http://www.methods.co.nz/asciidoc/) with
an equivalent sample of Markdown.  Here is a sample of
AsciiDoc from the AsciiDoc manual:

```
1. List item one.
+
List item one continued with a second paragraph followed by an
Indented block.
+
.................
$ ls *.sh
$ mv *.sh ~/tmp
.................
+
List item continued with a third paragraph.

2. List item two continued with an open block.
+
--
This paragraph is part of the preceding list item.

a. This list is nested and does not require explicit item
continuation.
+
This paragraph is part of the preceding list item.

b. List item b.

This paragraph belongs to item two of the outer list.
--
```

And here is the equivalent in Markdown:
```
1.  List item one.

    List item one continued with a second paragraph followed by an
    Indented block.

        $ ls *.sh
        $ mv *.sh ~/tmp

    List item continued with a third paragraph.

2.  List item two continued with an open block.

    This paragraph is part of the preceding list item.

    1. This list is nested and does not require explicit item continuation.

       This paragraph is part of the preceding list item.

    2. List item b.

    This paragraph belongs to item two of the outer list.
```

The AsciiDoc version is, arguably, easier to write. You don't need
to worry about indentation.  But the Markdown version is much easier
to read.  The nesting of list items is apparent to the eye in the
source, not just in the processed document.

## Why is a spec needed?

John Gruber's [canonical description of Markdown's
syntax](http://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/syntax)
does not specify the syntax unambiguously.  Here are some examples of
questions it does not answer:

1.  How much indentation is needed for a sublist?  The spec says that
    continuation paragraphs need to be indented four spaces, but is
    not fully explicit about sublists.  It is natural to think that
    they, too, must be indented four spaces, but `Markdown.pl` does
    not require that.  This is hardly a "corner case," and divergences
    between implementations on this issue often lead to surprises for
    users in real documents. (See [this comment by John
    Gruber](https://web.archive.org/web/20170611172104/http://article.gmane.org/gmane.text.markdown.general/1997).)

2.  Is a blank line needed before a block quote or heading?
    Most implementations do not require the blank line.  However,
    this can lead to unexpected results in hard-wrapped text, and
    also to ambiguities in parsing (note that some implementations
    put the heading inside the blockquote, while others do not).
    (John Gruber has also spoken [in favor of requiring the blank
    lines](https://web.archive.org/web/20170611172104/http://article.gmane.org/gmane.text.markdown.general/2146).)

3.  Is a blank line needed before an indented code block?
    (`Markdown.pl` requires it, but this is not mentioned in the
    documentation, and some implementations do not require it.)

    ``` markdown
    paragraph
        code?
    ```

4.  What is the exact rule for determining when list items get
    wrapped in `<p>` tags?  Can a list be partially "loose" and partially
    "tight"?  What should we do with a list like this?

    ``` markdown
    1. one

    2. two
    3. three
    ```

    Or this?

    ``` markdown
    1.  one
        - a

        - b
    2.  two
    ```

    (There are some relevant comments by John Gruber
    [here](https://web.archive.org/web/20170611172104/http://article.gmane.org/gmane.text.markdown.general/2554).)

5.  Can list markers be indented?  Can ordered list markers be right-aligned?

    ``` markdown
     8. item 1
     9. item 2
    10. item 2a
    ```

6.  Is this one list with a thematic break in its second item,
    or two lists separated by a thematic break?

    ``` markdown
    * a
    * * * * *
    * b
    ```

7.  When list markers change from numbers to bullets, do we have
    two lists or one?  (The Markdown syntax description suggests two,
    but the perl scripts and many other implementations produce one.)

    ``` markdown
    1. fee
    2. fie
    -  foe
    -  fum
    ```

8.  What are the precedence rules for the markers of inline structure?
    For example, is the following a valid link, or does the code span
    take precedence ?

    ``` markdown
    [a backtick (`)](/url) and [another backtick (`)](/url).
    ```

9.  What are the precedence rules for markers of emphasis and strong
    emphasis?  For example, how should the following be parsed?

    ``` markdown
    *foo *bar* baz*
    ```

10. What are the precedence rules between block-level and inline-level
    structure?  For example, how should the following be parsed?

    ``` markdown
    - `a long code span can contain a hyphen like this
      - and it can screw things up`
    ```

11. Can list items include section headings?  (`Markdown.pl` does not
    allow this, but does allow blockquotes to include headings.)

    ``` markdown
    - # Heading
    ```

12. Can list items be empty?

    ``` markdown
    * a
    *
    * b
    ```

13. Can link references be defined inside block quotes or list items?

    ``` markdown
    > Blockquote [foo].
    >
    > [foo]: /url
    ```

14. If there are multiple definitions for the same reference, which takes
    precedence?

    ``` markdown
    [foo]: /url1
    [foo]: /url2

    [foo][]
    ```

In the absence of a spec, early implementers consulted `Markdown.pl`
to resolve these ambiguities.  But `Markdown.pl` was quite buggy, and
gave manifestly bad results in many cases, so it was not a
satisfactory replacement for a spec.

Because there is no unambiguous spec, implementations have diverged
considerably.  As a result, users are often surprised to find that
a document that renders one way on one system (say, a GitHub wiki)
renders differently on another (say, converting to docbook using
pandoc).  To make matters worse, because nothing in Markdown counts
as a "syntax error," the divergence often isn't discovered right away.

## About this document

This document attempts to specify Markdown syntax unambiguously.
It contains many examples with side-by-side Markdown and
HTML.  These are intended to double as conformance tests.  An
accompanying script `spec_tests.py` can be used to run the tests
against any Markdown program:

    python test/spec_tests.py --spec spec.txt --program PROGRAM

Since this document describes how Markdown is to be parsed into
an abstract syntax tree, it would have made sense to use an abstract
representation of the syntax tree instead of HTML.  But HTML is capable
of representing the structural distinctions we need to make, and the
choice of HTML for the tests makes it possible to run the tests against
an implementation without writing an abstract syntax tree renderer.

This document is generated from a text file, `spec.txt`, written
in Markdown with a small extension for the side-by-side tests.
The script `tools/makespec.py` can be used to convert `spec.txt` into
HTML or CommonMark (which can then be converted into other formats).

In the examples, the `→` character is used to represent tabs.

# Preliminaries

## Characters and lines

Any sequence of [characters] is a valid CommonMark
document.

A [character](@) is a Unicode code point.  Although some
code points (for example, combining accents) do not correspond to
characters in an intuitive sense, all code points count as characters
for purposes of this spec.

This spec does not specify an encoding; it thinks of lines as composed
of [characters] rather than bytes.  A conforming parser may be limited
to a certain encoding.

A [line](@) is a sequence of zero or more [characters]
other than newline (`U+000A`) or carriage return (`U+000D`),
followed by a [line ending] or by the end of file.

A [line ending](@) is a newline (`U+000A`), a carriage return
(`U+000D`) not followed by a newline, or a carriage return and a
following newline.

A line containing no characters, or a line containing only spaces
(`U+0020`) or tabs (`U+0009`), is called a [blank line](@).

The following definitions of character classes will be used in this spec:

A [whitespace character](@) is a space
(`U+0020`), tab (`U+0009`), newline (`U+000A`), line tabulation (`U+000B`),
form feed (`U+000C`), or carriage return (`U+000D`).

[Whitespace](@) is a sequence of one or more [whitespace
characters].

A [Unicode whitespace character](@) is
any code point in the Unicode `Zs` general category, or a tab (`U+0009`),
carriage return (`U+000D`), newline (`U+000A`), or form feed
(`U+000C`).

[Unicode whitespace](@) is a sequence of one
or more [Unicode whitespace characters].

A [space](@) is `U+0020`.

A [non-whitespace character](@) is any character
that is not a [whitespace character].

An [ASCII punctuation character](@)
is `!`, `"`, `#`, `$`, `%`, `&`, `'`, `(`, `)`,
`*`, `+`, `,`, `-`, `.`, `/` (U+0021–2F), 
`:`, `;`, `<`, `=`, `>`, `?`, `@` (U+003A–0040),
`[`, `\`, `]`, `^`, `_`, `` ` `` (U+005B–0060), 
`{`, `|`, `}`, or `~` (U+007B–007E).

A [punctuation character](@) is an [ASCII
punctuation character] or anything in
the general Unicode categories  `Pc`, `Pd`, `Pe`, `Pf`, `Pi`, `Po`, or `Ps`.

## Tabs

Tabs in lines are not expanded to [spaces].  However,
in contexts where whitespace helps to define block structure,
tabs behave as if they were replaced by spaces with a tab stop
of 4 characters.

Thus, for example, a tab can be used instead of four spaces
in an indented code block.  (Note, however, that internal
tabs are passed through as literal tabs, not expanded to
spaces.)

```````````````````````````````` example
→foo→baz→→bim
.
Document scope=1:1..1:13 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:2..1:13 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="foo\tbaz\t\tbim\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````

```````````````````````````````` example
  →foo→baz→→bim
.
Document scope=1:1..1:15 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:4..1:15 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="foo\tbaz\t\tbim\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````

```````````````````````````````` example
    a→a
    ὐ→a
.
Document scope=1:1..2:9 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:5..2:9 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="a\ta\nὐ\ta\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````

In the following example, a continuation paragraph of a list
item is indented with a tab; this has exactly the same effect
as indentation with four spaces would:

```````````````````````````````` example
  - foo

→bar
.
Document scope=1:1..3:4 children=1
└── List scope=1:3..3:4 flavor=bullet start=null tight=false children=1
    └── ListItem scope=1:3..3:4 checked=null children=2
        ├── Paragraph scope=1:5..1:7 children=1
        │   └── Text scope=1:5..1:7 literal="foo" children=0
        └── Paragraph scope=3:2..3:4 children=1
            └── Text scope=3:2..3:4 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````

```````````````````````````````` example
- foo

→→bar
.
Document scope=1:1..3:5 children=1
└── List scope=1:1..3:5 flavor=bullet start=null tight=false children=1
    └── ListItem scope=1:1..3:5 checked=null children=2
        ├── Paragraph scope=1:3..1:5 children=1
        │   └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
        └── CodeBlock scope=3:2..3:5 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="  bar\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````

Normally the `>` that begins a block quote may be followed
optionally by a space, which is not considered part of the
content.  In the following case `>` is followed by a tab,
which is treated as if it were expanded into three spaces.
Since one of these spaces is considered part of the
delimiter, `foo` is considered to be indented six spaces
inside the block quote context, so we get an indented
code block starting with two spaces.

```````````````````````````````` example
>→→foo
.
Document scope=1:1..1:6 children=1
└── BlockQuote scope=1:1..1:6 children=1
    └── CodeBlock scope=1:3..1:6 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="  foo\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````

```````````````````````````````` example
-→→foo
.
Document scope=1:1..1:6 children=1
└── List scope=1:1..1:6 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=1
    └── ListItem scope=1:1..1:6 checked=null children=1
        └── CodeBlock scope=1:3..1:6 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="  foo\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
    foo
→bar
.
Document scope=1:1..2:4 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:5..2:4 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="foo\nbar\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````

```````````````````````````````` example
 - foo
   - bar
→ - baz
.
Document scope=1:1..3:7 children=1
└── List scope=1:2..3:7 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=1
    └── ListItem scope=1:2..3:7 checked=null children=2
        ├── Paragraph scope=1:4..1:6 children=1
        │   └── Text scope=1:4..1:6 literal="foo" children=0
        └── List scope=2:4..3:7 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=1
            └── ListItem scope=2:4..3:7 checked=null children=2
                ├── Paragraph scope=2:6..2:8 children=1
                │   └── Text scope=2:6..2:8 literal="bar" children=0
                └── List scope=3:3..3:7 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=1
                    └── ListItem scope=3:3..3:7 checked=null children=1
                        └── Paragraph scope=3:5..3:7 children=1
                            └── Text scope=3:5..3:7 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````

```````````````````````````````` example
#→Foo
.
Document scope=1:1..1:5 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..1:5 level=1 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="Foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````

```````````````````````````````` example
*→*→*→
.
Document scope=1:1..1:6 children=1
└── ThematicBreak scope=1:1..1:6 children=0
````````````````````````````````


## Insecure characters

For security reasons, the Unicode character `U+0000` must be replaced
with the REPLACEMENT CHARACTER (`U+FFFD`).

# Blocks and inlines

We can think of a document as a sequence of
[blocks](@)---structural elements like paragraphs, block
quotations, lists, headings, rules, and code blocks.  Some blocks (like
block quotes and list items) contain other blocks; others (like
headings and paragraphs) contain [inline](@) content---text,
links, emphasized text, images, code spans, and so on.

## Precedence

Indicators of block structure always take precedence over indicators
of inline structure.  So, for example, the following is a list with
two items, not a list with one item containing a code span:

```````````````````````````````` example
- `one
- two`
.
Document scope=1:1..2:6 children=1
└── List scope=1:1..2:6 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=2
    ├── ListItem scope=1:1..1:6 checked=null children=1
    │   └── Paragraph scope=1:3..1:6 children=1
    │       └── Text scope=1:4..1:6 literal="`one" children=0
    └── ListItem scope=2:1..2:6 checked=null children=1
        └── Paragraph scope=2:3..2:6 children=1
            └── Text scope=2:3..2:7 literal="two`" children=0
````````````````````````````````


This means that parsing can proceed in two steps:  first, the block
structure of the document can be discerned; second, text lines inside
paragraphs, headings, and other block constructs can be parsed for inline
structure.  The second step requires information about link reference
definitions that will be available only at the end of the first
step.  Note that the first step requires processing lines in sequence,
but the second can be parallelized, since the inline parsing of
one block element does not affect the inline parsing of any other.

## Container blocks and leaf blocks

We can divide blocks into two types:
[container blocks](@),
which can contain other blocks, and [leaf blocks](@),
which cannot.

# Leaf blocks

This section describes the different kinds of leaf block that make up a
Markdown document.

## Thematic breaks

A line consisting of 0-3 spaces of indentation, followed by a sequence
of three or more matching `-`, `_`, or `*` characters, each followed
optionally by any number of spaces or tabs, forms a
[thematic break](@).

```````````````````````````````` example
***
---
___
.
Document scope=1:1..3:3 children=3
├── ThematicBreak scope=1:1..1:3 children=0
├── ThematicBreak scope=2:1..2:3 children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=3:1..3:3 children=0
````````````````````````````````


Wrong characters:

```````````````````````````````` example
+++
.
Document scope=1:1..1:3 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="+++" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
===
.
Document scope=1:1..1:3 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="===" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Not enough characters:

```````````````````````````````` example
--
**
__
.
Document scope=1:1..3:2 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..3:2 children=5
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:2 literal="--" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    ├── Text scope=2:1..2:2 literal="**" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=3:1..3:2 literal="__" children=0
````````````````````````````````


One to three spaces indent are allowed:

```````````````````````````````` example
 ***
  ***
   ***
.
Document scope=1:1..3:6 children=3
├── ThematicBreak scope=1:2..1:4 children=0
├── ThematicBreak scope=2:3..2:5 children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=3:4..3:6 children=0
````````````````````````````````


Four spaces is too many:

```````````````````````````````` example
    ***
.
Document scope=1:1..1:7 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:5..1:7 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="***\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
    ***
.
Document scope=1:1..2:7 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:7 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="***" children=0
````````````````````````````````


More than three characters may be used:

```````````````````````````````` example
_____________________________________
.
Document scope=1:1..1:37 children=1
└── ThematicBreak scope=1:1..1:37 children=0
````````````````````````````````


Spaces are allowed between the characters:

```````````````````````````````` example
 - - -
.
Document scope=1:1..1:6 children=1
└── ThematicBreak scope=1:2..1:6 children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
 **  * ** * ** * **
.
Document scope=1:1..1:19 children=1
└── ThematicBreak scope=1:2..1:19 children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
-     -      -      -
.
Document scope=1:1..1:21 children=1
└── ThematicBreak scope=1:1..1:21 children=0
````````````````````````````````


Spaces are allowed at the end:

```````````````````````````````` example
- - - -    
.
Document scope=1:1..1:11 children=1
└── ThematicBreak scope=1:1..1:11 children=0
````````````````````````````````


However, no other characters may occur in the line:

```````````````````````````````` example
_ _ _ _ a

a------

---a---
.
Document scope=1:1..5:7 children=3
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:9 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:1..1:9 literal="_ _ _ _ a" children=0
├── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:7 children=1
│   └── Text scope=3:1..3:7 literal="a------" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=5:1..5:7 children=1
    └── Text scope=5:1..5:7 literal="---a---" children=0
````````````````````````````````


It is required that all of the [non-whitespace characters] be the same.
So, this is not a thematic break:

```````````````````````````````` example
 *-*
.
Document scope=1:1..1:4 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:2..1:4 children=1
    └── Emphasis scope=1:2..1:4 children=1
        └── Text scope=1:3..1:3 literal="-" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Thematic breaks do not need blank lines before or after:

```````````````````````````````` example
- foo
***
- bar
.
Document scope=1:1..3:5 children=3
├── List scope=1:1..1:5 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=1
│   └── ListItem scope=1:1..1:5 checked=null children=1
│       └── Paragraph scope=1:3..1:5 children=1
│           └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
├── ThematicBreak scope=2:1..2:3 children=0
└── List scope=3:1..3:5 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=1
    └── ListItem scope=3:1..3:5 checked=null children=1
        └── Paragraph scope=3:3..3:5 children=1
            └── Text scope=3:3..3:5 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Thematic breaks can interrupt a paragraph:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
***
bar
.
Document scope=1:1..3:3 children=3
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:3 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
├── ThematicBreak scope=2:1..2:3 children=0
└── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=3:1..3:3 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


If a line of dashes that meets the above conditions for being a
thematic break could also be interpreted as the underline of a [setext
heading], the interpretation as a
[setext heading] takes precedence. Thus, for example,
this is a setext heading, not a paragraph followed by a thematic break:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
---
bar
.
Document scope=1:1..3:3 children=2
├── Heading scope=1:1..3:3 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=3:1..3:3 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


When both a thematic break and a list item are possible
interpretations of a line, the thematic break takes precedence:

```````````````````````````````` example
* Foo
* * *
* Bar
.
Document scope=1:1..3:5 children=3
├── List scope=1:1..1:5 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=1
│   └── ListItem scope=1:1..1:5 checked=null children=1
│       └── Paragraph scope=1:3..1:5 children=1
│           └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="Foo" children=0
├── ThematicBreak scope=2:1..2:5 children=0
└── List scope=3:1..3:5 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=1
    └── ListItem scope=3:1..3:5 checked=null children=1
        └── Paragraph scope=3:3..3:5 children=1
            └── Text scope=3:3..3:5 literal="Bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


If you want a thematic break in a list item, use a different bullet:

```````````````````````````````` example
- Foo
- * * *
.
Document scope=1:1..2:7 children=1
└── List scope=1:1..2:7 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=2
    ├── ListItem scope=1:1..1:5 checked=null children=1
    │   └── Paragraph scope=1:3..1:5 children=1
    │       └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="Foo" children=0
    └── ListItem scope=2:1..2:7 checked=null children=1
        └── ThematicBreak scope=2:3..2:7 children=0
````````````````````````````````


## ATX headings

An [ATX heading](@)
consists of a string of characters, parsed as inline content, between an
opening sequence of 1--6 unescaped `#` characters and an optional
closing sequence of any number of unescaped `#` characters.
The opening sequence of `#` characters must be followed by a
[space] or by the end of line. The optional closing sequence of `#`s must be
preceded by a [space] and may be followed by spaces only.  The opening
`#` character may be indented 0-3 spaces.  The raw contents of the
heading are stripped of leading and trailing spaces before being parsed
as inline content.  The heading level is equal to the number of `#`
characters in the opening sequence.

Simple headings:

```````````````````````````````` example
# foo
## foo
### foo
#### foo
##### foo
###### foo
.
Document scope=1:1..6:10 children=6
├── Heading scope=1:1..1:5 level=1 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
├── Heading scope=2:1..2:6 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=2:4..2:6 literal="foo" children=0
├── Heading scope=3:1..3:7 level=3 children=1
│   └── Text scope=3:5..3:7 literal="foo" children=0
├── Heading scope=4:1..4:8 level=4 children=1
│   └── Text scope=4:6..4:8 literal="foo" children=0
├── Heading scope=5:1..5:9 level=5 children=1
│   └── Text scope=5:7..5:9 literal="foo" children=0
└── Heading scope=6:1..6:10 level=6 children=1
    └── Text scope=6:8..6:10 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


More than six `#` characters is not a heading:

```````````````````````````````` example
####### foo
.
Document scope=1:1..1:11 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:11 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:11 literal="####### foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


At least one space is required between the `#` characters and the
heading's contents, unless the heading is empty.  Note that many
implementations currently do not require the space.  However, the
space was required by the
[original ATX implementation](http://www.aaronsw.com/2002/atx/atx.py),
and it helps prevent things like the following from being parsed as
headings:

```````````````````````````````` example
#5 bolt

#hashtag
.
Document scope=1:1..3:8 children=2
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:7 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:1..1:7 literal="#5 bolt" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:8 children=1
    └── Text scope=3:1..3:8 literal="#hashtag" children=0
````````````````````````````````


This is not a heading, because the first `#` is escaped:

```````````````````````````````` example
\## foo
.
Document scope=1:1..1:7 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:7 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:7 literal="## foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Contents are parsed as inlines:

```````````````````````````````` example
# foo *bar* \*baz\*
.
Document scope=1:1..1:19 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..1:19 level=1 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:3..1:6 literal="foo " children=0
    ├── Emphasis scope=1:7..1:11 children=1
    │   └── Text scope=1:8..1:10 literal="bar" children=0
    └── Text scope=1:12..1:19 literal=" *baz*" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Leading and trailing [whitespace] is ignored in parsing inline content:

```````````````````````````````` example
#                  foo                     
.
Document scope=1:1..1:22 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..1:22 level=1 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:20..1:22 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


One to three spaces indentation are allowed:

```````````````````````````````` example
 ### foo
  ## foo
   # foo
.
Document scope=1:1..3:8 children=3
├── Heading scope=1:2..1:8 level=3 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:6..1:8 literal="foo" children=0
├── Heading scope=2:3..2:8 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=2:6..2:8 literal="foo" children=0
└── Heading scope=3:4..3:8 level=1 children=1
    └── Text scope=3:6..3:8 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Four spaces are too much:

```````````````````````````````` example
    # foo
.
Document scope=1:1..1:9 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:5..1:9 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="# foo\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
foo
    # bar
.
Document scope=1:1..2:9 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:9 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="foo" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:5 literal="# bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


A closing sequence of `#` characters is optional:

```````````````````````````````` example
## foo ##
  ###   bar    ###
.
Document scope=1:1..2:11 children=2
├── Heading scope=1:1..1:6 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:4..1:6 literal="foo" children=0
└── Heading scope=2:3..2:11 level=3 children=1
    └── Text scope=2:9..2:11 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


It need not be the same length as the opening sequence:

```````````````````````````````` example
# foo ##################################
##### foo ##
.
Document scope=1:1..2:9 children=2
├── Heading scope=1:1..1:5 level=1 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
└── Heading scope=2:1..2:9 level=5 children=1
    └── Text scope=2:7..2:9 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Spaces are allowed after the closing sequence:

```````````````````````````````` example
### foo ###     
.
Document scope=1:1..1:7 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..1:7 level=3 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:5..1:7 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


A sequence of `#` characters with anything but [spaces] following it
is not a closing sequence, but counts as part of the contents of the
heading:

```````````````````````````````` example
### foo ### b
.
Document scope=1:1..1:13 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..1:13 level=3 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:5..1:13 literal="foo ### b" children=0
````````````````````````````````


The closing sequence must be preceded by a space:

```````````````````````````````` example
# foo#
.
Document scope=1:1..1:6 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..1:6 level=1 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:3..1:6 literal="foo#" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Backslash-escaped `#` characters do not count as part
of the closing sequence:

```````````````````````````````` example
### foo \###
## foo #\##
# foo \#
.
Document scope=1:1..3:8 children=3
├── Heading scope=1:1..1:12 level=3 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:5..1:12 literal="foo ###" children=0
├── Heading scope=2:1..2:11 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=2:4..2:11 literal="foo ###" children=0
└── Heading scope=3:1..3:8 level=1 children=1
    └── Text scope=3:3..3:8 literal="foo #" children=0
````````````````````````````````


ATX headings need not be separated from surrounding content by blank
lines, and they can interrupt paragraphs:

```````````````````````````````` example
****
## foo
****
.
Document scope=1:1..3:4 children=3
├── ThematicBreak scope=1:1..1:4 children=0
├── Heading scope=2:1..2:6 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=2:4..2:6 literal="foo" children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=3:1..3:4 children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
Foo bar
# baz
Bar foo
.
Document scope=1:1..3:7 children=3
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:7 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:1..1:7 literal="Foo bar" children=0
├── Heading scope=2:1..2:5 level=1 children=1
│   └── Text scope=2:3..2:5 literal="baz" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:7 children=1
    └── Text scope=3:1..3:7 literal="Bar foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


ATX headings can be empty:

```````````````````````````````` example
## 
#
### ###
.
Document scope=1:1..3:3 children=3
├── Heading scope=1:1..1:3 level=2 children=0
├── Heading scope=2:1..2:1 level=1 children=0
└── Heading scope=3:1..3:3 level=3 children=0
````````````````````````````````


## Setext headings

A [setext heading](@) consists of one or more
lines of text, each containing at least one [non-whitespace
character], with no more than 3 spaces indentation, followed by
a [setext heading underline].  The lines of text must be such
that, were they not followed by the setext heading underline,
they would be interpreted as a paragraph:  they cannot be
interpretable as a [code fence], [ATX heading][ATX headings],
[block quote][block quotes], [thematic break][thematic breaks],
[list item][list items], or [HTML block][HTML blocks].

A [setext heading underline](@) is a sequence of
`=` characters or a sequence of `-` characters, with no more than 3
spaces of indentation and any number of trailing spaces or tabs.

The heading is a level 1 heading if `=` characters are used in
the [setext heading underline], and a level 2 heading if `-`
characters are used.  The contents of the heading are the result
of parsing the preceding lines of text as CommonMark inline
content.

In general, a setext heading need not be preceded or followed by a
blank line.  However, it cannot interrupt a paragraph, so when a
setext heading comes after a paragraph, a blank line is needed between
them.

Simple examples:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo *bar*
=========

Foo *bar*
---------
.
Document scope=1:1..5:9 children=2
├── Heading scope=1:1..3:0 level=1 children=2
│   ├── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="Foo " children=0
│   └── Emphasis scope=1:5..1:9 children=1
│       └── Text scope=1:6..1:8 literal="bar" children=0
└── Heading scope=4:1..5:9 level=2 children=2
    ├── Text scope=4:1..4:4 literal="Foo " children=0
    └── Emphasis scope=4:5..4:9 children=1
        └── Text scope=4:6..4:8 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


The content of the header may span more than one line:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo *bar
baz*
====
.
Document scope=1:1..3:4 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..3:4 level=1 children=2
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="Foo " children=0
    └── Emphasis scope=1:5..2:4 children=3
        ├── Text scope=1:6..1:8 literal="bar" children=0
        ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
        └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````

The contents are the result of parsing the headings's raw
content as inlines.  The heading's raw content is formed by
concatenating the lines and removing initial and final
[whitespace].

```````````````````````````````` example
  Foo *bar
baz*→
====
.
Document scope=1:1..3:4 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:3..3:4 level=1 children=2
    ├── Text scope=1:3..1:6 literal="Foo " children=0
    └── Emphasis scope=1:7..2:6 children=3
        ├── Text scope=1:8..1:10 literal="bar" children=0
        ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
        └── Text scope=2:3..2:5 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


The underlining can be any length:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
-------------------------

Foo
=
.
Document scope=1:1..5:1 children=2
├── Heading scope=1:1..3:0 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
└── Heading scope=4:1..5:1 level=1 children=1
    └── Text scope=4:1..4:3 literal="Foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


The heading content can be indented up to three spaces, and need
not line up with the underlining:

```````````````````````````````` example
   Foo
---

  Foo
-----

  Foo
  ===
.
Document scope=1:1..8:5 children=3
├── Heading scope=1:4..3:0 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:4..1:6 literal="Foo" children=0
├── Heading scope=4:3..6:0 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=4:3..4:5 literal="Foo" children=0
└── Heading scope=7:3..8:5 level=1 children=1
    └── Text scope=7:3..7:5 literal="Foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Four spaces indent is too much:

```````````````````````````````` example
    Foo
    ---

    Foo
---
.
Document scope=1:1..5:3 children=2
├── CodeBlock scope=1:5..4:7 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="Foo\n---\n\nFoo\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=5:1..5:3 children=0
````````````````````````````````


The setext heading underline can be indented up to three spaces, and
may have trailing spaces:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
   ----      
.
Document scope=1:1..2:13 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..2:13 level=2 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Four spaces is too much:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
    ---
.
Document scope=1:1..2:7 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:7 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="---" children=0
````````````````````````````````


The setext heading underline cannot contain internal spaces:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
= =

Foo
--- -
.
Document scope=1:1..5:5 children=3
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:3 children=3
│   ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
│   ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
│   └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="= =" children=0
├── Paragraph scope=4:1..4:3 children=1
│   └── Text scope=4:1..4:3 literal="Foo" children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=5:1..5:5 children=0
````````````````````````````````


Trailing spaces in the content line do not cause a line break:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo  
-----
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..2:5 level=2 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Nor does a backslash at the end:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo\
----
.
Document scope=1:1..2:4 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..2:4 level=2 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="Foo\\" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Since indicators of block structure take precedence over
indicators of inline structure, the following are setext headings:

```````````````````````````````` example
`Foo
----
`

<a title="a lot
---
of dashes"/>
.
Document scope=1:1..7:12 children=4
├── Heading scope=1:1..3:1 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:2..1:4 literal="`Foo" children=0
├── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:1 children=1
│   └── Text scope=3:2..3:2 literal="`" children=0
├── Heading scope=5:1..7:12 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=5:1..5:15 literal="<a title=\"a lot" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=7:1..7:12 children=1
    └── Text scope=7:1..7:12 literal="of dashes\"/>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


The setext heading underline cannot be a [lazy continuation
line] in a list item or block quote:

```````````````````````````````` example
> Foo
---
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=2
├── BlockQuote scope=1:1..1:5 children=1
│   └── Paragraph scope=1:3..1:5 children=1
│       └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="Foo" children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=2:1..2:3 children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
> foo
bar
===
.
Document scope=1:1..3:3 children=1
└── BlockQuote scope=1:1..3:3 children=1
    └── Paragraph scope=1:3..3:3 children=5
        ├── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
        ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
        ├── Text scope=2:3..2:5 literal="bar" children=0
        ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
        └── Text scope=3:3..3:5 literal="===" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
- Foo
---
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=2
├── List scope=1:1..1:5 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=1
│   └── ListItem scope=1:1..1:5 checked=null children=1
│       └── Paragraph scope=1:3..1:5 children=1
│           └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="Foo" children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=2:1..2:3 children=0
````````````````````````````````


A blank line is needed between a paragraph and a following
setext heading, since otherwise the paragraph becomes part
of the heading's content:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
Bar
---
.
Document scope=1:1..3:3 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..3:3 level=2 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="Bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


But in general a blank line is not required before or after
setext headings:

```````````````````````````````` example
---
Foo
---
Bar
---
Baz
.
Document scope=1:1..6:3 children=4
├── ThematicBreak scope=1:1..1:3 children=0
├── Heading scope=2:1..4:3 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="Foo" children=0
├── Heading scope=4:1..6:3 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=4:1..4:3 literal="Bar" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=6:1..6:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=6:1..6:3 literal="Baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Setext headings cannot be empty:

```````````````````````````````` example

====
.
Document scope=1:1..2:4 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=2:1..2:4 children=1
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:4 literal="====" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Setext heading text lines must not be interpretable as block
constructs other than paragraphs.  So, the line of dashes
in these examples gets interpreted as a thematic break:

```````````````````````````````` example
---
---
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=2
├── ThematicBreak scope=1:1..1:3 children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=2:1..2:3 children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
- foo
-----
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=2
├── List scope=1:1..1:5 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=1
│   └── ListItem scope=1:1..1:5 checked=null children=1
│       └── Paragraph scope=1:3..1:5 children=1
│           └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=2:1..2:5 children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
    foo
---
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=2
├── CodeBlock scope=1:5..1:7 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="foo\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=2:1..2:3 children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
> foo
-----
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=2
├── BlockQuote scope=1:1..1:5 children=1
│   └── Paragraph scope=1:3..1:5 children=1
│       └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=2:1..2:5 children=0
````````````````````````````````


If you want a heading with `> foo` as its literal text, you can
use backslash escapes:

```````````````````````````````` example
\> foo
------
.
Document scope=1:1..2:6 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..2:6 level=2 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:6 literal="> foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


**Compatibility note:**  Most existing Markdown implementations
do not allow the text of setext headings to span multiple lines.
But there is no consensus about how to interpret

``` markdown
Foo
bar
---
baz
```

One can find four different interpretations:

1. paragraph "Foo", heading "bar", paragraph "baz"
2. paragraph "Foo bar", thematic break, paragraph "baz"
3. paragraph "Foo bar --- baz"
4. heading "Foo bar", paragraph "baz"

We find interpretation 4 most natural, and interpretation 4
increases the expressive power of CommonMark, by allowing
multiline headings.  Authors who want interpretation 1 can
put a blank line after the first paragraph:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo

bar
---
baz
.
Document scope=1:1..5:3 children=3
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:3 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
├── Heading scope=3:1..5:3 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=3:1..3:3 literal="bar" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=5:1..5:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=5:1..5:3 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Authors who want interpretation 2 can put blank lines around
the thematic break,

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
bar

---

baz
.
Document scope=1:1..6:3 children=3
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:3 children=3
│   ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
│   ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
│   └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="bar" children=0
├── ThematicBreak scope=4:1..5:0 children=0
└── Paragraph scope=6:1..6:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=6:1..6:3 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


or use a thematic break that cannot count as a [setext heading
underline], such as

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
bar
* * *
baz
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=3
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:3 children=3
│   ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
│   ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
│   └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="bar" children=0
├── ThematicBreak scope=3:1..3:5 children=0
└── Paragraph scope=4:1..4:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=4:1..4:3 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Authors who want interpretation 3 can use backslash escapes:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
bar
\---
baz
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..4:3 children=7
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    ├── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="bar" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    ├── Text scope=3:1..3:4 literal="---" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=4:1..4:3 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


## Indented code blocks

An [indented code block](@) is composed of one or more
[indented chunks] separated by blank lines.
An [indented chunk](@) is a sequence of non-blank lines,
each indented four or more spaces. The contents of the code block are
the literal contents of the lines, including trailing
[line endings], minus four spaces of indentation.
An indented code block has no [info string].

An indented code block cannot interrupt a paragraph, so there must be
a blank line between a paragraph and a following indented code block.
(A blank line is not needed, however, between a code block and a following
paragraph.)

```````````````````````````````` example
    a simple
      indented code block
.
Document scope=1:1..2:25 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:5..2:25 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="a simple\n  indented code block\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


If there is any ambiguity between an interpretation of indentation
as a code block and as indicating that material belongs to a [list
item][list items], the list item interpretation takes precedence:

```````````````````````````````` example
  - foo

    bar
.
Document scope=1:1..3:7 children=1
└── List scope=1:3..3:7 flavor=bullet start=null tight=false children=1
    └── ListItem scope=1:3..3:7 checked=null children=2
        ├── Paragraph scope=1:5..1:7 children=1
        │   └── Text scope=1:5..1:7 literal="foo" children=0
        └── Paragraph scope=3:5..3:7 children=1
            └── Text scope=3:5..3:7 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
1.  foo

    - bar
.
Document scope=1:1..3:9 children=1
└── List scope=1:1..3:9 flavor=ordered start=1 tight=false children=1
    └── ListItem scope=1:1..3:9 checked=null children=2
        ├── Paragraph scope=1:5..1:7 children=1
        │   └── Text scope=1:5..1:7 literal="foo" children=0
        └── List scope=3:5..3:9 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=1
            └── ListItem scope=3:5..3:9 checked=null children=1
                └── Paragraph scope=3:7..3:9 children=1
                    └── Text scope=3:7..3:9 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````



The contents of a code block are literal text, and do not get parsed
as Markdown:

```````````````````````````````` example
    <a/>
    *hi*

    - one
.
Document scope=1:1..4:9 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:5..4:9 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="<a/>\n*hi*\n\n- one\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


Here we have three chunks separated by blank lines:

```````````````````````````````` example
    chunk1

    chunk2
  
 
 
    chunk3
.
Document scope=1:1..7:10 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:5..7:10 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="chunk1\n\nchunk2\n\n\n\nchunk3\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


Any initial spaces beyond four will be included in the content, even
in interior blank lines:

```````````````````````````````` example
    chunk1
      
      chunk2
.
Document scope=1:1..3:12 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:5..3:12 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="chunk1\n  \n  chunk2\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


An indented code block cannot interrupt a paragraph.  (This
allows hanging indents and the like.)

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo
    bar

.
Document scope=1:1..3:0 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:7 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="Foo" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


However, any non-blank line with fewer than four leading spaces ends
the code block immediately.  So a paragraph may occur immediately
after indented code:

```````````````````````````````` example
    foo
bar
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=2
├── CodeBlock scope=1:5..1:7 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="foo\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
└── Paragraph scope=2:1..2:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


And indented code can occur immediately before and after other kinds of
blocks:

```````````````````````````````` example
# Heading
    foo
Heading
------
    foo
----
.
Document scope=1:1..6:4 children=5
├── Heading scope=1:1..1:9 level=1 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:3..1:9 literal="Heading" children=0
├── CodeBlock scope=2:5..2:7 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="foo\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
├── Heading scope=3:1..5:7 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=3:1..3:7 literal="Heading" children=0
├── CodeBlock scope=5:5..5:7 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="foo\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
└── ThematicBreak scope=6:1..6:4 children=0
````````````````````````````````


The first line can be indented more than four spaces:

```````````````````````````````` example
        foo
    bar
.
Document scope=1:1..2:7 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:5..2:7 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="    foo\nbar\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


Blank lines preceding or following an indented code block
are not included in it:

```````````````````````````````` example

    
    foo
    

.
Document scope=1:1..5:0 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=3:5..5:0 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="foo\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


Trailing spaces are included in the code block's content:

```````````````````````````````` example
    foo  
.
Document scope=1:1..1:9 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:5..1:9 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="foo  \n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````



## Fenced code blocks

A [code fence](@) is a sequence
of at least three consecutive backtick characters (`` ` ``) or
tildes (`~`).  (Tildes and backticks cannot be mixed.)
A [fenced code block](@)
begins with a code fence, indented no more than three spaces.

The line with the opening code fence may optionally contain some text
following the code fence; this is trimmed of leading and trailing
whitespace and called the [info string](@). If the [info string] comes
after a backtick fence, it may not contain any backtick
characters.  (The reason for this restriction is that otherwise
some inline code would be incorrectly interpreted as the
beginning of a fenced code block.)

The content of the code block consists of all subsequent lines, until
a closing [code fence] of the same type as the code block
began with (backticks or tildes), and with at least as many backticks
or tildes as the opening code fence.  If the leading code fence is
indented N spaces, then up to N spaces of indentation are removed from
each line of the content (if present).  (If a content line is not
indented, it is preserved unchanged.  If it is indented less than N
spaces, all of the indentation is removed.)

The closing code fence may be indented up to three spaces, and may be
followed only by spaces, which are ignored.  If the end of the
containing block (or document) is reached and no closing code fence
has been found, the code block contains all of the lines after the
opening code fence until the end of the containing block (or
document).  (An alternative spec would require backtracking in the
event that a closing code fence is not found.  But this makes parsing
much less efficient, and there seems to be no real downside to the
behavior described here.)

A fenced code block may interrupt a paragraph, and does not require
a blank line either before or after.

The content of a code fence is treated as literal text, not parsed
as inlines.  The first word of the [info string] is typically used to
specify the language of the code sample, and rendered in the `class`
attribute of the `code` tag.  However, this spec does not mandate any
particular treatment of the [info string].

Here is a simple example with backticks:

```````````````````````````````` example
```
<
 >
```
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..4:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="<\n >\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


With tildes:

```````````````````````````````` example
~~~
<
 >
~~~
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..4:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="<\n >\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````

Fewer than three backticks is not enough:

```````````````````````````````` example
``
foo
``
.
Document scope=1:1..3:2 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..3:2 children=1
    └── Code scope=1:3..1:7 mode=embedded literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````

The closing code fence must use the same character as the opening
fence:

```````````````````````````````` example
```
aaa
~~~
```
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..4:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\n~~~\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
~~~
aaa
```
~~~
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..4:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\n```\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


The closing code fence must be at least as long as the opening fence:

```````````````````````````````` example
````
aaa
```
``````
.
Document scope=1:1..4:6 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..4:6 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\n```\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
~~~~
aaa
~~~
~~~~
.
Document scope=1:1..4:4 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..4:4 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\n~~~\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


Unclosed code blocks are closed by the end of the document
(or the enclosing [block quote][block quotes] or [list item][list items]):

```````````````````````````````` example
```
.
Document scope=1:1..1:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..1:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="" fenced=true closed=false children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
`````

```
aaa
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..4:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="\n```\naaa\n" fenced=true closed=false children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
> ```
> aaa

bbb
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=2
├── BlockQuote scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
│   └── CodeBlock scope=1:3..3:0 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\n" fenced=true closed=false children=0
└── Paragraph scope=4:1..4:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=4:1..4:3 literal="bbb" children=0
````````````````````````````````


A code block can have all empty lines as its content:

```````````````````````````````` example
```

  
```
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..4:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="\n  \n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


A code block can be empty:

```````````````````````````````` example
```
```
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..2:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


Fences can be indented.  If the opening fence is indented,
content lines will have equivalent opening indentation removed,
if present:

```````````````````````````````` example
 ```
 aaa
aaa
```
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:2..4:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\naaa\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
  ```
aaa
  aaa
aaa
  ```
.
Document scope=1:1..5:5 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:3..5:5 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\naaa\naaa\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
   ```
   aaa
    aaa
  aaa
   ```
.
Document scope=1:1..5:6 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:4..5:6 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\n aaa\naaa\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


Four spaces indentation produces an indented code block:

```````````````````````````````` example
    ```
    aaa
    ```
.
Document scope=1:1..3:7 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:5..3:7 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="```\naaa\n```\n" fenced=false closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


Closing fences may be indented by 0-3 spaces, and their indentation
need not match that of the opening fence:

```````````````````````````````` example
```
aaa
  ```
.
Document scope=1:1..3:5 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..3:5 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
   ```
aaa
  ```
.
Document scope=1:1..3:5 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:4..3:5 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


This is not a closing fence, because it is indented 4 spaces:

```````````````````````````````` example
```
aaa
    ```
.
Document scope=1:1..3:7 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..3:7 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\n    ```\n" fenced=true closed=false children=0
````````````````````````````````



Code fences (opening and closing) cannot contain internal spaces:

```````````````````````````````` example
``` ```
aaa
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:3 children=3
    ├── Code scope=1:4..1:4 mode=embedded literal=" " children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="aaa" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
~~~~~~
aaa
~~~ ~~
.
Document scope=1:1..3:6 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..3:6 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="aaa\n~~~ ~~\n" fenced=true closed=false children=0
````````````````````````````````


Fenced code blocks can interrupt paragraphs, and can be followed
directly by paragraphs, without a blank line between:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo
```
bar
```
baz
.
Document scope=1:1..5:3 children=3
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:3 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="foo" children=0
├── CodeBlock scope=2:1..4:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="bar\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
└── Paragraph scope=5:1..5:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=5:1..5:3 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Other blocks can also occur before and after fenced code blocks
without an intervening blank line:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo
---
~~~
bar
~~~
# baz
.
Document scope=1:1..6:5 children=3
├── Heading scope=1:1..3:3 level=2 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="foo" children=0
├── CodeBlock scope=3:1..5:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="bar\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
└── Heading scope=6:1..6:5 level=1 children=1
    └── Text scope=6:3..6:5 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


An [info string] can be provided after the opening code fence.
Although this spec doesn't mandate any particular treatment of
the info string, the first word is typically used to specify
the language of the code block. In HTML output, the language is
normally indicated by adding a class to the `code` element consisting
of `language-` followed by the language name.

```````````````````````````````` example
```ruby
def foo(x)
  return 3
end
```
.
Document scope=1:1..5:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..5:3 mode=standalone info="ruby" language="ruby" literal="def foo(x)\n  return 3\nend\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
~~~~    ruby startline=3 $%@#$
def foo(x)
  return 3
end
~~~~~~~
.
Document scope=1:1..5:7 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..5:7 mode=standalone info="ruby startline=3 $%@#$" language="ruby" literal="def foo(x)\n  return 3\nend\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
````;
````
.
Document scope=1:1..2:4 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..2:4 mode=standalone info=";" language=";" literal="" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


[Info strings] for backtick code blocks cannot contain backticks:

```````````````````````````````` example
``` aa ```
foo
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:3 children=3
    ├── Code scope=1:4..1:7 mode=embedded literal="aa" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


[Info strings] for tilde code blocks can contain backticks and tildes:

```````````````````````````````` example
~~~ aa ``` ~~~
foo
~~~
.
Document scope=1:1..3:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..3:3 mode=standalone info="aa ``` ~~~" language="aa" literal="foo\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````


Closing code fences cannot have [info strings]:

```````````````````````````````` example
```
``` aaa
```
.
Document scope=1:1..3:3 children=1
└── CodeBlock scope=1:1..3:3 mode=standalone info=null language=null literal="``` aaa\n" fenced=true closed=true children=0
````````````````````````````````



## HTML blocks

An [HTML block](@) is a group of lines that is treated
as raw HTML (and will not be escaped in HTML output).

There are seven kinds of [HTML block], which can be defined by their
start and end conditions.  The block begins with a line that meets a
[start condition](@) (after up to three spaces optional indentation).
It ends with the first subsequent line that meets a matching [end
condition](@), or the last line of the document, or the last line of
the [container block](#container-blocks) containing the current HTML
block, if no line is encountered that meets the [end condition].  If
the first line meets both the [start condition] and the [end
condition], the block will contain just that line.

1.  **Start condition:**  line begins with the string `<script`,
`<pre`, or `<style` (case-insensitive), followed by whitespace,
the string `>`, or the end of the line.\
**End condition:**  line contains an end tag
`</script>`, `</pre>`, or `</style>` (case-insensitive; it
need not match the start tag).

2.  **Start condition:** line begins with the string `<!--`.\
**End condition:**  line contains the string `-->`.

3.  **Start condition:** line begins with the string `<?`.\
**End condition:** line contains the string `?>`.

4.  **Start condition:** line begins with the string `<!`
followed by an uppercase ASCII letter.\
**End condition:** line contains the character `>`.

5.  **Start condition:**  line begins with the string
`<![CDATA[`.\
**End condition:** line contains the string `]]>`.

6.  **Start condition:** line begins with the string `<` or `</`
followed by one of the strings (case-insensitive) `address`,
`article`, `aside`, `base`, `basefont`, `blockquote`, `body`,
`caption`, `center`, `col`, `colgroup`, `dd`, `details`, `dialog`,
`dir`, `div`, `dl`, `dt`, `fieldset`, `figcaption`, `figure`,
`footer`, `form`, `frame`, `frameset`,
`h1`, `h2`, `h3`, `h4`, `h5`, `h6`, `head`, `header`, `hr`,
`html`, `iframe`, `legend`, `li`, `link`, `main`, `menu`, `menuitem`,
`nav`, `noframes`, `ol`, `optgroup`, `option`, `p`, `param`,
`section`, `summary`, `table`, `tbody`, `td`,
`tfoot`, `th`, `thead`, `title`, `tr`, `track`, `ul`, followed
by [whitespace], the end of the line, the string `>`, or
the string `/>`.\
**End condition:** line is followed by a [blank line].

7.  **Start condition:**  line begins with a complete [open tag]
(with any [tag name] other than `script`,
`style`, or `pre`) or a complete [closing tag],
followed only by [whitespace] or the end of the line.\
**End condition:** line is followed by a [blank line].

HTML blocks continue until they are closed by their appropriate
[end condition], or the last line of the document or other [container
block](#container-blocks).  This means any HTML **within an HTML
block** that might otherwise be recognised as a start condition will
be ignored by the parser and passed through as-is, without changing
the parser's state.

For instance, `<pre>` within a HTML block started by `<table>` will not affect
the parser state; as the HTML block was started in by start condition 6, it
will end at any blank line. This can be surprising:

```````````````````````````````` example
<table><tr><td>
<pre>
**Hello**,

_world_.
</pre>
</td></tr></table>
.
Document scope=1:1..7:18 children=3
├── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..3:10 literal="<table><tr><td>\n<pre>\n**Hello**,\n" children=0
├── Paragraph scope=5:1..6:6 children=4
│   ├── Emphasis scope=5:1..5:7 children=1
│   │   └── Text scope=5:2..5:6 literal="world" children=0
│   ├── Text scope=5:8..5:8 literal="." children=0
│   ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
│   └── HTML scope=6:1..6:6 literal="</pre>" children=0
└── HTMLBlock scope=7:1..7:18 literal="</td></tr></table>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````

In this case, the HTML block is terminated by the newline — the `**Hello**`
text remains verbatim — and regular parsing resumes, with a paragraph,
emphasised `world` and inline and block HTML following.

All types of [HTML blocks] except type 7 may interrupt
a paragraph.  Blocks of type 7 may not interrupt a paragraph.
(This restriction is intended to prevent unwanted interpretation
of long tags inside a wrapped paragraph as starting HTML blocks.)

Some simple examples follow.  Here are some basic HTML blocks
of type 6:

```````````````````````````````` example
<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
           hi
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

okay.
.
Document scope=1:1..9:5 children=2
├── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..7:8 literal="<table>\n  <tr>\n    <td>\n           hi\n    </td>\n  </tr>\n</table>\n" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=9:1..9:5 children=1
    └── Text scope=9:1..9:5 literal="okay." children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
 <div>
  *hello*
         <foo><a>
.
Document scope=1:1..3:17 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:2..3:17 literal=" <div>\n  *hello*\n         <foo><a>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


A block can also start with a closing tag:

```````````````````````````````` example
</div>
*foo*
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..2:5 literal="</div>\n*foo*\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Here we have two HTML blocks with a Markdown paragraph between them:

```````````````````````````````` example
<DIV CLASS="foo">

*Markdown*

</DIV>
.
Document scope=1:1..5:6 children=3
├── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..1:17 literal="<DIV CLASS=\"foo\">\n" children=0
├── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:10 children=1
│   └── Emphasis scope=3:1..3:10 children=1
│       └── Text scope=3:2..3:9 literal="Markdown" children=0
└── HTMLBlock scope=5:1..5:6 literal="</DIV>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


The tag on the first line can be partial, as long
as it is split where there would be whitespace:

```````````````````````````````` example
<div id="foo"
  class="bar">
</div>
.
Document scope=1:1..3:6 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..3:6 literal="<div id=\"foo\"\n  class=\"bar\">\n</div>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<div id="foo" class="bar
  baz">
</div>
.
Document scope=1:1..3:6 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..3:6 literal="<div id=\"foo\" class=\"bar\n  baz\">\n</div>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


An open tag need not be closed:
```````````````````````````````` example
<div>
*foo*

*bar*
.
Document scope=1:1..4:5 children=2
├── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..2:5 literal="<div>\n*foo*\n" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=4:1..4:5 children=1
    └── Emphasis scope=4:1..4:5 children=1
        └── Text scope=4:2..4:4 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````



A partial tag need not even be completed (garbage
in, garbage out):

```````````````````````````````` example
<div id="foo"
*hi*
.
Document scope=1:1..2:4 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..2:4 literal="<div id=\"foo\"\n*hi*\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<div class
foo
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..2:3 literal="<div class\nfoo\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


The initial tag doesn't even need to be a valid
tag, as long as it starts like one:

```````````````````````````````` example
<div *???-&&&-<---
*foo*
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..2:5 literal="<div *???-&&&-<---\n*foo*\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


In type 6 blocks, the initial tag need not be on a line by
itself:

```````````````````````````````` example
<div><a href="bar">*foo*</a></div>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:34 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..1:34 literal="<div><a href=\"bar\">*foo*</a></div>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<table><tr><td>
foo
</td></tr></table>
.
Document scope=1:1..3:18 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..3:18 literal="<table><tr><td>\nfoo\n</td></tr></table>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Everything until the next blank line or end of document
gets included in the HTML block.  So, in the following
example, what looks like a Markdown code block
is actually part of the HTML block, which continues until a blank
line or the end of the document is reached:

```````````````````````````````` example
<div></div>
``` c
int x = 33;
```
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..4:3 literal="<div></div>\n``` c\nint x = 33;\n```\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


To start an [HTML block] with a tag that is *not* in the
list of block-level tags in (6), you must put the tag by
itself on the first line (and it must be complete):

```````````````````````````````` example
<a href="foo">
*bar*
</a>
.
Document scope=1:1..3:4 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..3:4 literal="<a href=\"foo\">\n*bar*\n</a>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


In type 7 blocks, the [tag name] can be anything:

```````````````````````````````` example
<Warning>
*bar*
</Warning>
.
Document scope=1:1..3:10 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..3:10 literal="<Warning>\n*bar*\n</Warning>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<i class="foo">
*bar*
</i>
.
Document scope=1:1..3:4 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..3:4 literal="<i class=\"foo\">\n*bar*\n</i>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
</ins>
*bar*
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..2:5 literal="</ins>\n*bar*\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


These rules are designed to allow us to work with tags that
can function as either block-level or inline-level tags.
The `<del>` tag is a nice example.  We can surround content with
`<del>` tags in three different ways.  In this case, we get a raw
HTML block, because the `<del>` tag is on a line by itself:

```````````````````````````````` example
<del>
*foo*
</del>
.
Document scope=1:1..3:6 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..3:6 literal="<del>\n*foo*\n</del>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


In this case, we get a raw HTML block that just includes
the `<del>` tag (because it ends with the following blank
line).  So the contents get interpreted as CommonMark:

```````````````````````````````` example
<del>

*foo*

</del>
.
Document scope=1:1..5:6 children=3
├── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..1:5 literal="<del>\n" children=0
├── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:5 children=1
│   └── Emphasis scope=3:1..3:5 children=1
│       └── Text scope=3:2..3:4 literal="foo" children=0
└── HTMLBlock scope=5:1..5:6 literal="</del>\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Finally, in this case, the `<del>` tags are interpreted
as [raw HTML] *inside* the CommonMark paragraph.  (Because
the tag is not on a line by itself, we get inline HTML
rather than an [HTML block].)

```````````````````````````````` example
<del>*foo*</del>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:16 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:16 children=3
    ├── HTML scope=1:1..1:5 literal="<del>" children=0
    ├── Emphasis scope=1:6..1:10 children=1
    │   └── Text scope=1:7..1:9 literal="foo" children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:11..1:16 literal="</del>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


HTML tags designed to contain literal content
(`script`, `style`, `pre`), comments, processing instructions,
and declarations are treated somewhat differently.
Instead of ending at the first blank line, these blocks
end at the first line containing a corresponding end tag.
As a result, these blocks can contain blank lines:

A pre tag (type 1):

```````````````````````````````` example
<pre language="haskell"><code>
import Text.HTML.TagSoup

main :: IO ()
main = print $ parseTags tags
</code></pre>
okay
.
Document scope=1:1..7:4 children=2
├── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..5:29 literal="<pre language=\"haskell\"><code>\nimport Text.HTML.TagSoup\n\nmain :: IO ()\nmain = print $ parseTags tags\n</code></pre>\n" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=7:1..7:4 children=1
    └── Text scope=7:1..7:4 literal="okay" children=0
````````````````````````````````


A script tag (type 1):

```````````````````````````````` example
<script type="text/javascript">
// JavaScript example

document.getElementById("demo").innerHTML = "Hello JavaScript!";
</script>
okay
.
Document scope=1:1..6:4 children=2
├── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..4:64 literal="<script type=\"text/javascript\">\n// JavaScript example\n\ndocument.getElementById(\"demo\").innerHTML = \"Hello JavaScript!\";\n</script>\n" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=6:1..6:4 children=1
    └── Text scope=6:1..6:4 literal="okay" children=0
````````````````````````````````


A style tag (type 1):

```````````````````````````````` example
<style
  type="text/css">
h1 {color:red;}

p {color:blue;}
</style>
okay
.
Document scope=1:1..7:4 children=2
├── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..5:15 literal="<style\n  type=\"text/css\">\nh1 {color:red;}\n\np {color:blue;}\n</style>\n" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=7:1..7:4 children=1
    └── Text scope=7:1..7:4 literal="okay" children=0
````````````````````````````````


If there is no matching end tag, the block will end at the
end of the document (or the enclosing [block quote][block quotes]
or [list item][list items]):

```````````````````````````````` example
<style
  type="text/css">

foo
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=1
└── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..4:3 literal="<style\n  type=\"text/css\">\n\nfoo\n" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
> <div>
> foo

bar
.
Document scope=1:1..4:3 children=2
├── BlockQuote scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
│   └── HTMLBlock scope=1:3..2:5 literal="<div>\nfoo\n" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=4:1..4:3 children=1
    └── Text scope=4:1..4:3 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
- <div>
- foo
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
└── List scope=1:1..2:5 flavor=bullet start=null tight=true children=2
    ├── ListItem scope=1:1..1:7 checked=null children=1
    │   └── HTMLBlock scope=1:3..1:7 literal="<div>\n" children=0
    └── ListItem scope=2:1..2:5 checked=null children=1
        └── Paragraph scope=2:3..2:5 children=1
            └── Text scope=2:3..2:5 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


The end tag can occur on the same line as the start tag:

```````````````````````````````` example
<style>p{color:red;}</style>
*foo*
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=2
├── HTMLBlock scope=1:1..0:0 literal="<style>p{color:red;}</style>\n" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=2:1..2:5 children=1
    └── Emphasis scope=2:1..2:5 children=1
        └── Text scope=2:2..2:4 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````
