the above example, the alt attribute's value is `foo bar`, not `foo
[bar](/url)` or `foo <a href="/url">bar</a>`.  Only the plain string
content is rendered, without formatting.

```````````````````````````````` example
![foo *bar*][]

[foo *bar*]: train.jpg "train & tracks"
.
Document scope=1:1..3:39 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:14 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:14 source="train.jpg" title="train & tracks" children=2
        ├── Text scope=1:3..1:6 literal="foo " children=0
        └── Emphasis scope=1:7..1:11 children=1
            └── Text scope=1:8..1:10 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
![foo *bar*][foobar]

[FOOBAR]: train.jpg "train & tracks"
.
Document scope=1:1..3:36 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:20 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:20 source="train.jpg" title="train & tracks" children=2
        ├── Text scope=1:3..1:6 literal="foo " children=0
        └── Emphasis scope=1:7..1:11 children=1
            └── Text scope=1:8..1:10 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
![foo](train.jpg)
.
Document scope=1:1..1:17 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:17 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:17 source="train.jpg" title=null children=1
        └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
My ![foo bar](/path/to/train.jpg  "title"   )
.
Document scope=1:1..1:45 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:45 children=2
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="My " children=0
    └── Image scope=1:4..1:45 source="/path/to/train.jpg" title="title" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:6..1:12 literal="foo bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
![foo](<url>)
.
Document scope=1:1..1:13 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:13 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:13 source="url" title=null children=1
        └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
![](/url)
.
Document scope=1:1..1:9 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:9 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:9 source="/url" title=null children=0
````````````````````````````````


Reference-style:

```````````````````````````````` example
![foo][bar]

[bar]: /url
.
Document scope=1:1..3:11 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:11 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:11 source="/url" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
![foo][bar]

[BAR]: /url
.
Document scope=1:1..3:11 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:11 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:11 source="/url" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Collapsed:

```````````````````````````````` example
![foo][]

[foo]: /url "title"
.
Document scope=1:1..3:19 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:8 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:8 source="/url" title="title" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
![*foo* bar][]

[*foo* bar]: /url "title"
.
Document scope=1:1..3:25 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:14 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:14 source="/url" title="title" children=2
        ├── Emphasis scope=1:3..1:7 children=1
        │   └── Text scope=1:4..1:6 literal="foo" children=0
        └── Text scope=1:8..1:11 literal=" bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


The labels are case-insensitive:

```````````````````````````````` example
![Foo][]

[foo]: /url "title"
.
Document scope=1:1..3:19 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:8 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:8 source="/url" title="title" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="Foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


As with reference links, [whitespace] is not allowed
between the two sets of brackets:

```````````````````````````````` example
![foo] 
[]

[foo]: /url "title"
.
Document scope=1:1..4:19 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:2 children=4
    ├── Image scope=1:1..1:6 source="/url" title="title" children=1
    │   └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
    ├── Text scope=1:7..1:7 literal="" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:2 literal="[]" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Shortcut:

```````````````````````````````` example
![foo]

[foo]: /url "title"
.
Document scope=1:1..3:19 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:6 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:6 source="/url" title="title" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
![*foo* bar]

[*foo* bar]: /url "title"
.
Document scope=1:1..3:25 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:12 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:12 source="/url" title="title" children=2
        ├── Emphasis scope=1:3..1:7 children=1
        │   └── Text scope=1:4..1:6 literal="foo" children=0
        └── Text scope=1:8..1:11 literal=" bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Note that link labels cannot contain unescaped brackets:

```````````````````````````````` example
![[foo]]

[[foo]]: /url "title"
.
Document scope=1:1..3:21 children=2
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:8 children=1
│   └── Text scope=1:1..1:8 literal="![[foo]]" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:21 children=1
    └── Text scope=3:1..3:21 literal="[[foo]]: /url \"title\"" children=0
````````````````````````````````


The link labels are case-insensitive:

```````````````````````````````` example
![Foo]

[foo]: /url "title"
.
Document scope=1:1..3:19 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:6 children=1
    └── Image scope=1:1..1:6 source="/url" title="title" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:3..1:5 literal="Foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


If you just want a literal `!` followed by bracketed text, you can
backslash-escape the opening `[`:

```````````````````````````````` example
!\[foo]

[foo]: /url "title"
.
Document scope=1:1..3:19 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:7 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:7 literal="![foo]" children=0
````````````````````````````````


If you want a link after a literal `!`, backslash-escape the
`!`:

```````````````````````````````` example
\![foo]

[foo]: /url "title"
.
Document scope=1:1..3:19 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:7 children=2
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:2 literal="!" children=0
    └── Link scope=1:3..1:7 destination="/url" title="title" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:4..1:6 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


## Autolinks

[Autolink](@)s are absolute URIs and email addresses inside
`<` and `>`. They are parsed as links, with the URL or email address
as the link label.

A [URI autolink](@) consists of `<`, followed by an
[absolute URI] followed by `>`.  It is parsed as
a link to the URI, with the URI as the link's label.

An [absolute URI](@),
for these purposes, consists of a [scheme] followed by a colon (`:`)
followed by zero or more characters other than ASCII
[whitespace] and control characters, `<`, and `>`.  If
the URI includes these characters, they must be percent-encoded
(e.g. `%20` for a space).

For purposes of this spec, a [scheme](@) is any sequence
of 2--32 characters beginning with an ASCII letter and followed
by any combination of ASCII letters, digits, or the symbols plus
("+"), period ("."), or hyphen ("-").

Here are some valid autolinks:

```````````````````````````````` example
<http://foo.bar.baz>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:20 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:20 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:20 destination="http://foo.bar.baz" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:2..1:19 literal="http://foo.bar.baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<http://foo.bar.baz/test?q=hello&id=22&boolean>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:47 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:47 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:47 destination="http://foo.bar.baz/test?q=hello&id=22&boolean" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:2..1:46 literal="http://foo.bar.baz/test?q=hello&id=22&boolean" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<irc://foo.bar:2233/baz>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:24 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:24 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:24 destination="irc://foo.bar:2233/baz" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:2..1:23 literal="irc://foo.bar:2233/baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Uppercase is also fine:

```````````````````````````````` example
<MAILTO:FOO@BAR.BAZ>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:20 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:20 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:20 destination="MAILTO:FOO@BAR.BAZ" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:2..1:19 literal="MAILTO:FOO@BAR.BAZ" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Note that many strings that count as [absolute URIs] for
purposes of this spec are not valid URIs, because their
schemes are not registered or because of other problems
with their syntax:

```````````````````````````````` example
<a+b+c:d>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:9 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:9 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:9 destination="a+b+c:d" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:2..1:8 literal="a+b+c:d" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<made-up-scheme://foo,bar>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:26 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:26 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:26 destination="made-up-scheme://foo,bar" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:2..1:25 literal="made-up-scheme://foo,bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<http://../>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:12 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:12 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:12 destination="http://../" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:2..1:11 literal="http://../" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<localhost:5001/foo>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:20 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:20 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:20 destination="localhost:5001/foo" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:2..1:19 literal="localhost:5001/foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Spaces are not allowed in autolinks:

```````````````````````````````` example
<http://foo.bar/baz bim>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:24 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:24 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:24 literal="<http://foo.bar/baz bim>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Backslash-escapes do not work inside autolinks:

```````````````````````````````` example
<http://example.com/\[\>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:24 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:24 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:24 destination="http://example.com/\\[\\" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:2..1:23 literal="http://example.com/\\[\\" children=0
````````````````````````````````


An [email autolink](@)
consists of `<`, followed by an [email address],
followed by `>`.  The link's label is the email address,
and the URL is `mailto:` followed by the email address.

An [email address](@),
for these purposes, is anything that matches
the [non-normative regex from the HTML5
spec](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/forms.html#e-mail-state-(type=email)):

    /^[a-zA-Z0-9.!#$%&'*+/=?^_`{|}~-]+@[a-zA-Z0-9](?:[a-zA-Z0-9-]{0,61}[a-zA-Z0-9])?
    (?:\.[a-zA-Z0-9](?:[a-zA-Z0-9-]{0,61}[a-zA-Z0-9])?)*$/

Examples of email autolinks:

```````````````````````````````` example
<foo@bar.example.com>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:21 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:21 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:21 destination="mailto:foo@bar.example.com" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:2..1:20 literal="foo@bar.example.com" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<foo+special@Bar.baz-bar0.com>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:30 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:30 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:30 destination="mailto:foo+special@Bar.baz-bar0.com" title="" children=1
        └── Text scope=1:2..1:29 literal="foo+special@Bar.baz-bar0.com" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Backslash-escapes do not work inside email autolinks:

```````````````````````````````` example
<foo\+@bar.example.com>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:23 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:23 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:23 literal="<foo+@bar.example.com>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


These are not autolinks:

```````````````````````````````` example
<>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:2 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:2 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:2 literal="<>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
< http://foo.bar >
.
Document scope=1:1..1:18 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:18 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:18 literal="< http://foo.bar >" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<m:abc>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:7 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:7 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:7 literal="<m:abc>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<foo.bar.baz>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:13 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:13 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:13 literal="<foo.bar.baz>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
http://example.com
.
Document scope=1:1..1:18 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:18 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:18 literal="http://example.com" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
foo@bar.example.com
.
Document scope=1:1..1:19 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:19 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:19 literal="foo@bar.example.com" children=0
````````````````````````````````

<div class="extension">

## Autolinks (extension)

GFM enables the `autolink` extension, where autolinks will be recognised in a
greater number of conditions.

[Autolink]s can also be constructed without requiring the use of `<` and to `>`
to delimit them, although they will be recognized under a smaller set of
circumstances.  All such recognized autolinks can only come at the beginning of
a line, after whitespace, or any of the delimiting characters `*`, `_`, `~`,
and `(`.

An [extended www autolink](@) will be recognized
when the text `www.` is found followed by a [valid domain].
A [valid domain](@) consists of segments
of alphanumeric characters, underscores (`_`) and hyphens (`-`)
separated by periods (`.`).
There must be at least one period,
and no underscores may be present in the last two segments of the domain.

The scheme `http` will be inserted automatically:

```````````````````````````````` example autolink
www.commonmark.org
.
Document scope=1:1..1:18 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:18 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:18 destination="http://www.commonmark.org" title=null children=1
        └── Text scope=1:1..1:18 literal="www.commonmark.org" children=0
````````````````````````````````

After a [valid domain], zero or more non-space non-`<` characters may follow:

```````````````````````````````` example autolink
Visit www.commonmark.org/help for more information.
.
Document scope=1:1..1:51 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:51 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:6 literal="Visit " children=0
    ├── Link scope=1:7..1:29 destination="http://www.commonmark.org/help" title=null children=1
    │   └── Text scope=1:7..1:29 literal="www.commonmark.org/help" children=0
    └── Text scope=1:30..1:51 literal=" for more information." children=0
````````````````````````````````

We then apply [extended autolink path validation](@) as follows:

Trailing punctuation (specifically, `?`, `!`, `.`, `,`, `:`, `*`, `_`, and `~`)
will not be considered part of the autolink, though they may be included in the
interior of the link:

```````````````````````````````` example autolink
Visit www.commonmark.org.

Visit www.commonmark.org/a.b.
.
Document scope=1:1..3:29 children=2
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:25 children=3
│   ├── Text scope=1:1..1:6 literal="Visit " children=0
│   ├── Link scope=1:7..1:24 destination="http://www.commonmark.org" title=null children=1
│   │   └── Text scope=1:7..1:24 literal="www.commonmark.org" children=0
│   └── Text scope=1:25..1:25 literal="." children=0
└── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:29 children=3
    ├── Text scope=3:1..3:6 literal="Visit " children=0
    ├── Link scope=3:7..3:28 destination="http://www.commonmark.org/a.b" title=null children=1
    │   └── Text scope=3:7..3:28 literal="www.commonmark.org/a.b" children=0
    └── Text scope=3:29..3:29 literal="." children=0
````````````````````````````````

When an autolink ends in `)`, we scan the entire autolink for the total number
of parentheses.  If there is a greater number of closing parentheses than
opening ones, we don't consider the unmatched trailing parentheses part of the
autolink, in order to facilitate including an autolink inside a parenthesis:

```````````````````````````````` example autolink
www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)

www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)))

(www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business))

(www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)
.
Document scope=1:1..7:42 children=4
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:41 children=1
│   └── Link scope=1:1..1:41 destination="http://www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)" title=null children=1
│       └── Text scope=1:1..1:41 literal="www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)" children=0
├── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:43 children=2
│   ├── Link scope=3:1..3:41 destination="http://www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)" title=null children=1
│   │   └── Text scope=3:1..3:41 literal="www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)" children=0
│   └── Text scope=3:42..3:43 literal="))" children=0
├── Paragraph scope=5:1..5:43 children=3
│   ├── Text scope=5:1..5:1 literal="(" children=0
│   ├── Link scope=5:2..5:42 destination="http://www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)" title=null children=1
│   │   └── Text scope=5:2..5:42 literal="www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)" children=0
│   └── Text scope=5:43..5:43 literal=")" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=7:1..7:42 children=2
    ├── Text scope=7:1..7:1 literal="(" children=0
    └── Link scope=7:2..7:42 destination="http://www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)" title=null children=1
        └── Text scope=7:2..7:42 literal="www.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)" children=0
````````````````````````````````

This check is only done when the link ends in a closing parentheses `)`, so if
the only parentheses are in the interior of the autolink, no special rules are
applied:

```````````````````````````````` example autolink
www.google.com/search?q=(business))+ok
.
Document scope=1:1..1:38 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:38 children=1
    └── Link scope=1:1..1:38 destination="http://www.google.com/search?q=(business))+ok" title=null children=1
        └── Text scope=1:1..1:38 literal="www.google.com/search?q=(business))+ok" children=0
````````````````````````````````

If an autolink ends in a semicolon (`;`), we check to see if it appears to
resemble an [entity reference][entity references]; if the preceding text is `&`
followed by one or more alphanumeric characters.  If so, it is excluded from
the autolink:

```````````````````````````````` example autolink
www.google.com/search?q=commonmark&hl=en

www.google.com/search?q=commonmark&hl;
.
Document scope=1:1..3:38 children=2
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:40 children=1
│   └── Link scope=1:1..1:40 destination="http://www.google.com/search?q=commonmark&hl=en" title=null children=1
│       └── Text scope=1:1..1:40 literal="www.google.com/search?q=commonmark&hl=en" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:38 children=2
    ├── Link scope=3:1..3:34 destination="http://www.google.com/search?q=commonmark" title=null children=1
    │   └── Text scope=3:1..3:34 literal="www.google.com/search?q=commonmark" children=0
    └── Text scope=3:35..3:38 literal="&hl;" children=0
````````````````````````````````

`<` immediately ends an autolink.

```````````````````````````````` example autolink
www.commonmark.org/he<lp
.
Document scope=1:1..1:24 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:24 children=2
    ├── Link scope=1:1..1:21 destination="http://www.commonmark.org/he" title=null children=1
    │   └── Text scope=1:1..1:21 literal="www.commonmark.org/he" children=0
    └── Text scope=1:22..1:24 literal="<lp" children=0
````````````````````````````````

An [extended url autolink](@) will be recognised when one of the schemes
`http://`, `https://`, or `ftp://`, followed by a [valid domain], then zero or
more non-space non-`<` characters according to
[extended autolink path validation]:

```````````````````````````````` example autolink
http://commonmark.org

(Visit https://encrypted.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business))

Anonymous FTP is available at ftp://foo.bar.baz.
.
Document scope=1:1..5:48 children=3
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:21 children=2
│   ├── Text scope=0:0..0:0 literal="" children=0
│   └── Link scope=1:1..1:21 destination="http://commonmark.org" title=null children=1
│       └── Text scope=1:1..1:21 literal="http://commonmark.org" children=0
├── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:63 children=3
│   ├── Text scope=3:1..3:7 literal="(Visit " children=0
│   ├── Link scope=3:8..3:62 destination="https://encrypted.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)" title=null children=1
│   │   └── Text scope=3:8..3:62 literal="https://encrypted.google.com/search?q=Markup+(business)" children=0
│   └── Text scope=3:63..3:63 literal=")" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=5:1..5:48 children=3
    ├── Text scope=5:1..5:30 literal="Anonymous FTP is available at " children=0
    ├── Link scope=5:31..5:47 destination="ftp://foo.bar.baz" title=null children=1
    │   └── Text scope=5:31..5:47 literal="ftp://foo.bar.baz" children=0
    └── Text scope=5:48..5:48 literal="." children=0
````````````````````````````````


An [extended email autolink](@) will be recognised when an email address is
recognised within any text node.  Email addresses are recognised according to
the following rules:

* One ore more characters which are alphanumeric, or `.`, `-`, `_`, or `+`.
* An `@` symbol.
* One or more characters which are alphanumeric, or `-` or `_`,
  separated by periods (`.`).
  There must be at least one period.
  The last character must not be one of `-` or `_`.

The scheme `mailto:` will automatically be added to the generated link:

```````````````````````````````` example autolink
foo@bar.baz
.
Document scope=1:1..1:11 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:11 children=3
    ├── Text scope=0:0..0:0 literal="" children=0
    ├── Link scope=1:1..1:11 destination="mailto:foo@bar.baz" title=null children=1
    │   └── Text scope=1:1..1:11 literal="foo@bar.baz" children=0
    └── Text scope=0:0..0:0 literal="" children=0
````````````````````````````````

`+` can occur before the `@`, but not after.

```````````````````````````````` example autolink
hello@mail+xyz.example isn't valid, but hello+xyz@mail.example is.
.
Document scope=1:1..1:66 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:66 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:40 literal="hello@mail+xyz.example isn't valid, but " children=0
    ├── Link scope=1:41..1:62 destination="mailto:hello+xyz@mail.example" title=null children=1
    │   └── Text scope=1:41..1:62 literal="hello+xyz@mail.example" children=0
    └── Text scope=1:63..1:66 literal=" is." children=0
````````````````````````````````

`.`, `-`, and `_` can occur on both sides of the `@`, but only `.` may occur at
the end of the email address, in which case it will not be considered part of
the address:

```````````````````````````````` example autolink
a.b-c_d@a.b

a.b-c_d@a.b.

a.b-c_d@a.b-

a.b-c_d@a.b_
.
Document scope=1:1..7:12 children=4
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:11 children=3
│   ├── Text scope=0:0..0:0 literal="" children=0
│   ├── Link scope=1:1..1:11 destination="mailto:a.b-c_d@a.b" title=null children=1
│   │   └── Text scope=1:1..1:11 literal="a.b-c_d@a.b" children=0
│   └── Text scope=0:0..0:0 literal="" children=0
├── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:12 children=3
│   ├── Text scope=0:0..0:0 literal="" children=0
│   ├── Link scope=3:1..3:11 destination="mailto:a.b-c_d@a.b" title=null children=1
│   │   └── Text scope=3:1..3:11 literal="a.b-c_d@a.b" children=0
│   └── Text scope=3:12..3:12 literal="." children=0
├── Paragraph scope=5:1..5:12 children=1
│   └── Text scope=5:1..5:12 literal="a.b-c_d@a.b-" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=7:1..7:12 children=1
    └── Text scope=7:1..7:12 literal="a.b-c_d@a.b_" children=0
````````````````````````````````

</div>

## Raw HTML

Text between `<` and `>` that looks like an HTML tag is parsed as a
raw HTML tag and will be rendered in HTML without escaping.
Tag and attribute names are not limited to current HTML tags,
so custom tags (and even, say, DocBook tags) may be used.

Here is the grammar for tags:

A [tag name](@) consists of an ASCII letter
followed by zero or more ASCII letters, digits, or
hyphens (`-`).

An [attribute](@) consists of [whitespace],
an [attribute name], and an optional
[attribute value specification].

An [attribute name](@)
consists of an ASCII letter, `_`, or `:`, followed by zero or more ASCII
letters, digits, `_`, `.`, `:`, or `-`.  (Note:  This is the XML
specification restricted to ASCII.  HTML5 is laxer.)

An [attribute value specification](@)
consists of optional [whitespace],
a `=` character, optional [whitespace], and an [attribute
value].

An [attribute value](@)
consists of an [unquoted attribute value],
a [single-quoted attribute value], or a [double-quoted attribute value].

An [unquoted attribute value](@)
is a nonempty string of characters not
including [whitespace], `"`, `'`, `=`, `<`, `>`, or `` ` ``.

A [single-quoted attribute value](@)
consists of `'`, zero or more
characters not including `'`, and a final `'`.

A [double-quoted attribute value](@)
consists of `"`, zero or more
characters not including `"`, and a final `"`.

An [open tag](@) consists of a `<` character, a [tag name],
zero or more [attributes], optional [whitespace], an optional `/`
character, and a `>` character.

A [closing tag](@) consists of the string `</`, a
[tag name], optional [whitespace], and the character `>`.

An [HTML comment](@) consists of `<!-->`, `<!--->`, or  `<!--`, a string of
characters not including the string `-->`, and `-->` (see the
[HTML spec](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/parsing.html#markup-declaration-open-state)).

A [processing instruction](@)
consists of the string `<?`, a string
of characters not including the string `?>`, and the string
`?>`.

A [declaration](@) consists of the
string `<!`, a name consisting of one or more uppercase ASCII letters,
[whitespace], a string of characters not including the
character `>`, and the character `>`.

A [CDATA section](@) consists of
the string `<![CDATA[`, a string of characters not including the string
`]]>`, and the string `]]>`.

An [HTML tag](@) consists of an [open tag], a [closing tag],
an [HTML comment], a [processing instruction], a [declaration],
or a [CDATA section].

Here are some simple open tags:

```````````````````````````````` example
<a><bab><c2c>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:13 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:13 children=3
    ├── HTML scope=1:1..1:3 literal="<a>" children=0
    ├── HTML scope=1:4..1:8 literal="<bab>" children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:9..1:13 literal="<c2c>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Empty elements:

```````````````````````````````` example
<a/><b2/>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:9 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:9 children=2
    ├── HTML scope=1:1..1:4 literal="<a/>" children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:5..1:9 literal="<b2/>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


[Whitespace] is allowed:

```````````````````````````````` example
<a  /><b2
data="foo" >
.
Document scope=1:1..2:12 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:12 children=2
    ├── HTML scope=1:1..1:6 literal="<a  />" children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:7..1:22 literal="<b2\ndata=\"foo\" >" children=0
````````````````````````````````


With attributes:

```````````````````````````````` example
<a foo="bar" bam = 'baz <em>"</em>'
_boolean zoop:33=zoop:33 />
.
Document scope=1:1..2:27 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:27 children=1
    └── HTML scope=1:1..1:63 literal="<a foo=\"bar\" bam = 'baz <em>\"</em>'\n_boolean zoop:33=zoop:33 />" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Custom tag names can be used:

```````````````````````````````` example
Foo <responsive-image src="foo.jpg" />
.
Document scope=1:1..1:38 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:38 children=2
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="Foo " children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:5..1:38 literal="<responsive-image src=\"foo.jpg\" />" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Illegal tag names, not parsed as HTML:

```````````````````````````````` example
<33> <__>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:9 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:9 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:9 literal="<33> <__>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Illegal attribute names:

```````````````````````````````` example
<a h*#ref="hi">
.
Document scope=1:1..1:15 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:15 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:15 literal="<a h*#ref=\"hi\">" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Illegal attribute values:

```````````````````````````````` example
<a href="hi'> <a href=hi'>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:26 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:26 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:26 literal="<a href=\"hi'> <a href=hi'>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Illegal [whitespace]:

```````````````````````````````` example
< a><
foo><bar/ >
<foo bar=baz
bim!bop />
.
Document scope=1:1..4:10 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..4:10 children=7
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:5 literal="< a><" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    ├── Text scope=2:1..2:11 literal="foo><bar/ >" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    ├── Text scope=3:1..3:12 literal="<foo bar=baz" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=4:1..4:10 literal="bim!bop />" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Missing [whitespace]:

```````````````````````````````` example
<a href='bar'title=title>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:25 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:25 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:25 literal="<a href='bar'title=title>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Closing tags:

```````````````````````````````` example
</a></foo >
.
Document scope=1:1..1:11 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:11 children=2
    ├── HTML scope=1:1..1:4 literal="</a>" children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:5..1:11 literal="</foo >" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Illegal attributes in closing tag:

```````````````````````````````` example
</a href="foo">
.
Document scope=1:1..1:15 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:15 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:15 literal="</a href=\"foo\">" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Comments:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo <!-- this is a --
comment - with hyphens -->
.
Document scope=1:1..2:26 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:26 children=2
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="foo " children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:5..1:48 literal="<!-- this is a --\ncomment - with hyphens -->" children=0
````````````````````````````````

```````````````````````````````` example
foo <!--> foo -->

foo <!---> foo -->
.
Document scope=1:1..3:18 children=2
├── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:17 children=3
│   ├── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="foo " children=0
│   ├── HTML scope=1:5..1:9 literal="<!-->" children=0
│   └── Text scope=1:10..1:17 literal=" foo -->" children=0
└── Paragraph scope=3:1..3:18 children=3
    ├── Text scope=3:1..3:4 literal="foo " children=0
    ├── HTML scope=3:5..3:10 literal="<!--->" children=0
    └── Text scope=3:11..3:18 literal=" foo -->" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Processing instructions:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo <?php echo $a; ?>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:21 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:21 children=2
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="foo " children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:5..1:21 literal="<?php echo $a; ?>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Declarations:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo <!ELEMENT br EMPTY>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:23 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:23 children=2
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="foo " children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:5..1:23 literal="<!ELEMENT br EMPTY>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


CDATA sections:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo <![CDATA[>&<]]>
.
Document scope=1:1..1:19 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:19 children=2
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="foo " children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:5..1:19 literal="<![CDATA[>&<]]>" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Entity and numeric character references are preserved in HTML
attributes:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo <a href="&ouml;">
.
Document scope=1:1..1:21 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:21 children=2
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="foo " children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:5..1:21 literal="<a href=\"&ouml;\">" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Backslash escapes do not work in HTML attributes:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo <a href="\*">
.
Document scope=1:1..1:17 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:17 children=2
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="foo " children=0
    └── HTML scope=1:5..1:17 literal="<a href=\"\\*\">" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<a href="\"">
.
Document scope=1:1..1:13 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:13 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:13 literal="<a href=\"\"\">" children=0
````````````````````````````````


<div class="extension">

## Disallowed Raw HTML (extension)

GFM enables the `tagfilter` extension, where the following HTML tags will be
filtered when rendering HTML output:

* `<title>`
* `<textarea>`
* `<style>`
* `<xmp>`
* `<iframe>`
* `<noembed>`
* `<noframes>`
* `<script>`
* `<plaintext>`

Filtering is done by replacing the leading `<` with the entity `&lt;`.  These
tags are chosen in particular as they change how HTML is interpreted in a way
unique to them (i.e. nested HTML is interpreted differently), and this is
usually undesireable in the context of other rendered Markdown content.

All other HTML tags are left untouched.

</div>

## Hard line breaks

A line break (not in a code span or HTML tag) that is preceded
by two or more spaces and does not occur at the end of a block
is parsed as a [hard line break](@) (rendered
in HTML as a `<br />` tag):

```````````````````````````````` example
foo  
baz
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:3 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
    ├── LineBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


For a more visible alternative, a backslash before the
[line ending] may be used instead of two spaces:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo\
baz
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:3 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="foo" children=0
    ├── LineBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=1:6..1:8 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


More than two spaces can be used:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo       
baz
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:3 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:10 literal="foo" children=0
    ├── LineBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Leading spaces at the beginning of the next line are ignored:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo  
     bar
.
Document scope=1:1..2:8 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:8 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:5 literal="foo" children=0
    ├── LineBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
foo\
     bar
.
Document scope=1:1..2:8 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:8 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="foo" children=0
    ├── LineBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=1:6..1:8 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Line breaks can occur inside emphasis, links, and other constructs
that allow inline content:

```````````````````````````````` example
*foo  
bar*
.
Document scope=1:1..2:4 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:4 children=1
    └── Emphasis scope=1:1..2:4 children=3
        ├── Text scope=1:2..1:6 literal="foo" children=0
        ├── LineBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
        └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
*foo\
bar*
.
Document scope=1:1..2:4 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:4 children=1
    └── Emphasis scope=1:1..1:10 children=3
        ├── Text scope=1:2..1:4 literal="foo" children=0
        ├── LineBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
        └── Text scope=1:7..1:9 literal="bar" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Line breaks do not occur inside code spans

```````````````````````````````` example
`code  
span`
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
    └── Code scope=1:2..1:12 mode=embedded literal="code   span" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
`code\
span`
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
    └── Code scope=1:2..1:11 mode=embedded literal="code\\ span" children=0
````````````````````````````````


or HTML tags:

```````````````````````````````` example
<a href="foo  
bar">
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
    └── HTML scope=1:1..1:20 literal="<a href=\"foo  \nbar\">" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
<a href="foo\
bar">
.
Document scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:5 children=1
    └── HTML scope=1:1..1:19 literal="<a href=\"foo\\\nbar\">" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Hard line breaks are for separating inline content within a block.
Neither syntax for hard line breaks works at the end of a paragraph or
other block element:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo\
.
Document scope=1:1..1:4 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:4 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="foo\\" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
foo  
.
Document scope=1:1..1:5 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:5 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
### foo\
.
Document scope=1:1..1:8 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..1:8 level=3 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:5..1:8 literal="foo\\" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
### foo  
.
Document scope=1:1..1:7 children=1
└── Heading scope=1:1..1:7 level=3 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:5..1:7 literal="foo" children=0
````````````````````````````````


## Soft line breaks

A regular line break (not in a code span or HTML tag) that is not
preceded by two or more spaces or a backslash is parsed as a
[softbreak](@).  (A softbreak may be rendered in HTML either as a
[line ending] or as a space. The result will be the same in
browsers. In the examples here, a [line ending] will be used.)

```````````````````````````````` example
foo
baz
.
Document scope=1:1..2:3 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:3 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:3 literal="foo" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Spaces at the end of the line and beginning of the next line are
removed:

```````````````````````````````` example
foo 
 baz
.
Document scope=1:1..2:4 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..2:4 children=3
    ├── Text scope=1:1..1:4 literal="foo" children=0
    ├── SoftBreak scope=0:0..0:0 children=0
    └── Text scope=2:1..2:3 literal="baz" children=0
````````````````````````````````


A conforming parser may render a soft line break in HTML either as a
line break or as a space.

A renderer may also provide an option to render soft line breaks
as hard line breaks.

## Textual content

Any characters not given an interpretation by the above rules will
be parsed as plain textual content.

```````````````````````````````` example
hello $.;'there
.
Document scope=1:1..1:15 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:15 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:15 literal="hello $.;'there" children=0
````````````````````````````````


```````````````````````````````` example
Foo χρῆν
.
Document scope=1:1..1:13 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:13 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:13 literal="Foo χρῆν" children=0
````````````````````````````````


Internal spaces are preserved verbatim:

```````````````````````````````` example
Multiple     spaces
.
Document scope=1:1..1:19 children=1
└── Paragraph scope=1:1..1:19 children=1
    └── Text scope=1:1..1:19 literal="Multiple     spaces" children=0
````````````````````````````````


<!-- END TESTS -->

# Appendix: A parsing strategy

In this appendix we describe some features of the parsing strategy
used in the CommonMark reference implementations.

## Overview

Parsing has two phases:

1. In the first phase, lines of input are consumed and the block
structure of the document---its division into paragraphs, block quotes,
list items, and so on---is constructed.  Text is assigned to these
blocks but not parsed. Link reference definitions are parsed and a
map of links is constructed.

2. In the second phase, the raw text contents of paragraphs and headings
are parsed into sequences of Markdown inline elements (strings,
code spans, links, emphasis, and so on), using the map of link
references constructed in phase 1.

At each point in processing, the document is represented as a tree of
**blocks**.  The root of the tree is a `document` block.  The `document`
may have any number of other blocks as **children**.  These children
may, in turn, have other blocks as children.  The last child of a block
is normally considered **open**, meaning that subsequent lines of input
can alter its contents.  (Blocks that are not open are **closed**.)
Here, for example, is a possible document tree, with the open blocks
marked by arrows:

``` tree
-> document
  -> block_quote
       paragraph
         "Lorem ipsum dolor\nsit amet."
    -> list (type=bullet tight=true bullet_char=-)
         list_item
           paragraph
             "Qui *quodsi iracundia*"
      -> list_item
        -> paragraph
             "aliquando id"
```

## Phase 1: block structure

Each line that is processed has an effect on this tree.  The line is
analyzed and, depending on its contents, the document may be altered
in one or more of the following ways:

1. One or more open blocks may be closed.
2. One or more new blocks may be created as children of the
   last open block.
3. Text may be added to the last (deepest) open block remaining
   on the tree.

Once a line has been incorporated into the tree in this way,
it can be discarded, so input can be read in a stream.

For each line, we follow this procedure:

1. First we iterate through the open blocks, starting with the
root document, and descending through last children down to the last
open block.  Each block imposes a condition that the line must satisfy
if the block is to remain open.  For example, a block quote requires a
`>` character.  A paragraph requires a non-blank line.
In this phase we may match all or just some of the open
blocks.  But we cannot close unmatched blocks yet, because we may have a
[lazy continuation line].

2.  Next, after consuming the continuation markers for existing
blocks, we look for new block starts (e.g. `>` for a block quote).
If we encounter a new block start, we close any blocks unmatched
in step 1 before creating the new block as a child of the last
matched block.

3.  Finally, we look at the remainder of the line (after block
markers like `>`, list markers, and indentation have been consumed).
This is text that can be incorporated into the last open
block (a paragraph, code block, heading, or raw HTML).

Setext headings are formed when we see a line of a paragraph
that is a [setext heading underline].

Reference link definitions are detected when a paragraph is closed;
the accumulated text lines are parsed to see if they begin with
one or more reference link definitions.  Any remainder becomes a
normal paragraph.

We can see how this works by considering how the tree above is
generated by four lines of Markdown:

``` markdown
> Lorem ipsum dolor
sit amet.
> - Qui *quodsi iracundia*
> - aliquando id
```

At the outset, our document model is just

``` tree
-> document
```

The first line of our text,

``` markdown
> Lorem ipsum dolor
```

causes a `block_quote` block to be created as a child of our
open `document` block, and a `paragraph` block as a child of
the `block_quote`.  Then the text is added to the last open
block, the `paragraph`:

``` tree
-> document
  -> block_quote
    -> paragraph
         "Lorem ipsum dolor"
```

The next line,

``` markdown
sit amet.
```

is a "lazy continuation" of the open `paragraph`, so it gets added
to the paragraph's text:

``` tree
-> document
  -> block_quote
    -> paragraph
         "Lorem ipsum dolor\nsit amet."
```

The third line,

``` markdown
> - Qui *quodsi iracundia*
```

causes the `paragraph` block to be closed, and a new `list` block
opened as a child of the `block_quote`.  A `list_item` is also
added as a child of the `list`, and a `paragraph` as a child of
the `list_item`.  The text is then added to the new `paragraph`:

``` tree
-> document
  -> block_quote
       paragraph
         "Lorem ipsum dolor\nsit amet."
    -> list (type=bullet tight=true bullet_char=-)
      -> list_item
        -> paragraph
             "Qui *quodsi iracundia*"
```

The fourth line,

``` markdown
> - aliquando id
```

causes the `list_item` (and its child the `paragraph`) to be closed,
and a new `list_item` opened up as child of the `list`.  A `paragraph`
is added as a child of the new `list_item`, to contain the text.
We thus obtain the final tree:

``` tree
-> document
  -> block_quote
       paragraph
         "Lorem ipsum dolor\nsit amet."
    -> list (type=bullet tight=true bullet_char=-)
         list_item
           paragraph
             "Qui *quodsi iracundia*"
      -> list_item
        -> paragraph
             "aliquando id"
```

## Phase 2: inline structure

Once all of the input has been parsed, all open blocks are closed.

We then "walk the tree," visiting every node, and parse raw
string contents of paragraphs and headings as inlines.  At this
point we have seen all the link reference definitions, so we can
resolve reference links as we go.

``` tree
document
  block_quote
    paragraph
      str "Lorem ipsum dolor"
      softbreak
      str "sit amet."
    list (type=bullet tight=true bullet_char=-)
      list_item
        paragraph
          str "Qui "
          emph
            str "quodsi iracundia"
      list_item
        paragraph
          str "aliquando id"
```

Notice how the [line ending] in the first paragraph has
been parsed as a `softbreak`, and the asterisks in the first list item
have become an `emph`.

### An algorithm for parsing nested emphasis and links

By far the trickiest part of inline parsing is handling emphasis,
strong emphasis, links, and images.  This is done using the following
algorithm.

When we're parsing inlines and we hit either

- a run of `*` or `_` characters, or
- a `[` or `![`

we insert a text node with these symbols as its literal content, and we
add a pointer to this text node to the [delimiter stack](@).

The [delimiter stack] is a doubly linked list.  Each
element contains a pointer to a text node, plus information about

- the type of delimiter (`[`, `![`, `*`, `_`)
- the number of delimiters,
- whether the delimiter is "active" (all are active to start), and
- whether the delimiter is a potential opener, a potential closer,
  or both (which depends on what sort of characters precede
  and follow the delimiters).

When we hit a `]` character, we call the *look for link or image*
procedure (see below).

When we hit the end of the input, we call the *process emphasis*
procedure (see below), with `stack_bottom` = NULL.

#### *look for link or image*

Starting at the top of the delimiter stack, we look backwards
through the stack for an opening `[` or `![` delimiter.

- If we don't find one, we return a literal text node `]`.

- If we do find one, but it's not *active*, we remove the inactive
  delimiter from the stack, and return a literal text node `]`.

- If we find one and it's active, then we parse ahead to see if
  we have an inline link/image, reference link/image, compact reference
  link/image, or shortcut reference link/image.

  + If we don't, then we remove the opening delimiter from the
    delimiter stack and return a literal text node `]`.

  + If we do, then

    * We return a link or image node whose children are the inlines
      after the text node pointed to by the opening delimiter.

    * We run *process emphasis* on these inlines, with the `[` opener
      as `stack_bottom`.

    * We remove the opening delimiter.

    * If we have a link (and not an image), we also set all
      `[` delimiters before the opening delimiter to *inactive*.  (This
      will prevent us from getting links within links.)

#### *process emphasis*

Parameter `stack_bottom` sets a lower bound to how far we
descend in the [delimiter stack].  If it is NULL, we can
go all the way to the bottom.  Otherwise, we stop before
visiting `stack_bottom`.

Let `current_position` point to the element on the [delimiter stack]
just above `stack_bottom` (or the first element if `stack_bottom`
is NULL).

We keep track of the `openers_bottom` for each delimiter
type (`*`, `_`) and each length of the closing delimiter run
(modulo 3).  Initialize this to `stack_bottom`.

Then we repeat the following until we run out of potential
closers:

- Move `current_position` forward in the delimiter stack (if needed)
  until we find the first potential closer with delimiter `*` or `_`.
  (This will be the potential closer closest
  to the beginning of the input -- the first one in parse order.)

- Now, look back in the stack (staying above `stack_bottom` and
  the `openers_bottom` for this delimiter type) for the
  first matching potential opener ("matching" means same delimiter).

- If one is found:

  + Figure out whether we have emphasis or strong emphasis:
    if both closer and opener spans have length >= 2, we have
    strong, otherwise regular.

  + Insert an emph or strong emph node accordingly, after
    the text node corresponding to the opener.

  + Remove any delimiters between the opener and closer from
    the delimiter stack.

  + Remove 1 (for regular emph) or 2 (for strong emph) delimiters
    from the opening and closing text nodes.  If they become empty
    as a result, remove them and remove the corresponding element
    of the delimiter stack.  If the closing node is removed, reset
    `current_position` to the next element in the stack.

- If none is found:

  + Set `openers_bottom` to the element before `current_position`.
    (We know that there are no openers for this kind of closer up to and
    including this point, so this puts a lower bound on future searches.)

  + If the closer at `current_position` is not a potential opener,
    remove it from the delimiter stack (since we know it can't
    be a closer either).

  + Advance `current_position` to the next element in the stack.

After we're done, we remove all delimiters above `stack_bottom` from the
delimiter stack.
